bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump's dismissal of Iran 's response as "totally unacceptable" and "playing games," emphasizing demands like war compensation and Hormuz sovereignty to portray US obstructionism, while right-leaning outlets tout the US "peace proposal" as a strength signal, highlighting Iran's "militarily defeated" status and Ayatollah's "readiness" meetings with suspicion via scare quotes.
Left employs critical tones critiquing Trump personally; right uses optimistic phrasing like "signals willingness" amid tensions.
Pivotal divides include Trump's defeat claim versus rejection rhetoric .
Center differs via neutral source attributions like "state media.
All agree on the response via Pakistan aiming to end the two-month war, underscoring divides in US leverage versus Iranian legitimacy.
summary: Iran has sent its response to a US proposal aimed at ending the more than two-month war through mediator Pakistan, according to Iran's IRNA news agency and CNBC.

The initial focus of the proposed plan is to end the war and maintain shipping security by allowing traffic through the Strait of Hormuz while negotiating a fuller deal including disputes like Iran's nuclear program later.
The peace efforts seek a temporary memorandum of understanding to halt the war before addressing broader disputes.
Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian stated that dialogue does not imply surrender, and the Revolutionary Guards have warned of targeting US sites if Iranian tankers are attacked.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the 3-day ceasefire and 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner swap with skepticism via terms like "says" and "request," subordinating Trump's role while emphasizing "Russian invasion" and Victory Day as a "gift to Putin," evoking caution and fragility from past violations.
Right-Leaning coverage celebrates it as a "Diplomatic Win" and "surprise" breakthrough, highlighting Trump's announcement as the "beginning of the end" with triumphant tones.
Pivotal divides emerge on Trump's mediation—de-emphasized left, elevated right—and Victory Day's implications.
Center-Leaning neutrally "announces" and "confirms" without qualifiers.
All agree on dates, swaps, and Zelenskyy/Ushakov validations, revealing ideological rifts over leadership efficacy and aggression narratives.
summary: US President Donald Trump announced a three-day ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine from May 9 to May 11 following US-mediated talks and stated that peace negotiations were progressing.
Trump confirmed the ceasefire on social media, emphasizing the specific dates of May 9, 10, and 11 and that both sides agreed to it.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky confirmed the US role in mediating the ceasefire and announced an exchange of 1,000 prisoners between Russia and Ukraine.
The ceasefire aligns with Russia's Victory Day on May 9, but humanitarian concerns persist while Russia warned against disruptions to its Victory Day parade.
title: Trump Announces Three-Day Russia-Ukraine Ceasefire, 1,000-Prisoner Swap

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the ruling as a "devastating blow" that "nullifies the will" of voters, portraying the court as "conservative-dominated.
Conversely, right-leaning media celebrates "killing" a "corrupt scheme" and "unconstitutional power grab," using terms like "nixed" to signal a win for "election integrity.
While the right highlights the "wasted" $70 million spent on the effort, the left de-emphasizes costs to focus on countering partisan imbalances.
Center-Leaning reports highlight the "major blow" to midterm hopes.
Both sides agree the 4-3 decision reinstates a 6-5 map, reflecting broader conflicts between judicial procedure and democratic mandates.
summary: On Friday, May 8, 2026, the Virginia Supreme Court struck down a voter-approved redistricting amendment in a 4-3 decision, ruling the Democratic-led General Assembly violated procedural requirements in placing the measure on the ballot.

Justices found the legislature failed to comply with constitutional sequencing requirements when the initial vote occurred after early voting for the 2025 general election had already commenced, violating state constitutional mandates.

Blocking the Democratic-drawn plan preserves the 2021 congressional map, maintaining Virginia's current 6-5 Democratic advantage instead of the proposed 10-1 margin that Democrats sought to gain up to four House seats.

Certification remains halted as state election officials navigate delays that complicate candidate filings and primary preparations, with the May 25 filing deadline now imminent and uncertainty over district boundaries persisting.

The ruling intensifies the national redistricting battle ahead of the 2026 midterm elections , as both parties maneuver for electoral advantage in the narrowly divided House while Republican-led states accelerate their own map changes.
title: Virginia Supreme Court strikes down Democrats’ redistricting plan, dimming party’s midterm hopes

summary: On Friday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported U.S. employers added 115,000 payroll jobs in April, surpassing economist expectations of 65,000 while the unemployment rate remained steady at 4.3%.
Labor Department revisions reduced February and March payrolls by a net 16,000 jobs, while the ongoing U.S. war with Iran strains energy supplies and elevates fuel costs above $4.50 per gallon.
Healthcare companies led hiring with 37,000 new positions, while average hourly earnings rose 3.6% year-over-year, consistent with Federal Reserve inflation targets despite energy price pressures.

Resilient payroll data reinforces expectations that the Federal Reserve will maintain current interest rates, as policymakers weigh steady employment growth against persistent inflation from the Middle East conflict.

Lower immigration and an aging population have reduced the economy's break-even employment point to near zero, meaning fewer than 10,000 new jobs monthly may now sustain stable unemployment—a trend economists describe as unprecedented in recent history.
title: U.S. payrolls increased 115,000 in April, more than expected; unemployment at 4.3%

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame U.S. strikes as a "dramatic escalation" under the mocking "Peace President," emphasizing " ceasefire violation" and Iranian claims of tanker and civilian hits, while right-leaning ones portray them as vital "self-defense" after unprovoked Iranian assaults on named destroyers, celebrating attackers "destroyed" and rockets that "slam" foes amid a "so-called" truce.
Left employs chaotic, skeptical tones like "blows up"; right uses triumphant phrasing such as "love tap.
Pivotal divides include U.S. port strikes—touted as restrained wins by right, reckless by left—and Trump's threats.
Center coverage balances reciprocity without partisan flair.
Both agree on the Strait exchange, no U.S. damage, and claimed ceasefire endurance, underscoring divides over American restraint versus resolve.
summary: On Thursday, May 7, 2026, U.S. Central Command forces intercepted unprovoked Iranian attacks on three U.S. Navy destroyers transiting the Strait of Hormuz and launched self-defense strikes against Iranian military facilities.
Iranian military officials claimed the U.S. violated the fragile ceasefire in effect since April 7 by targeting an oil tanker and coastal areas including Qeshm Island before the naval confrontation.

Shipping data firm Lloyd's List Intelligence reported Thursday that Iran established a 'Persian Gulf Strait Authority' to vet vessels and collect tolls, complicating navigation for hundreds of commercial ships trapped in the Persian Gulf.

President Donald Trump described the retaliatory strikes as a 'love tap' to maintain the ceasefire, while the United Arab Emirates reported its air defenses engaged missile and drone attacks early Friday.

Global energy markets face uncertainty as approximately 20% of world petroleum supply transits the waterway; benchmark Brent crude jumped above $100 per barrel following the clashes, heightening concerns over supply disruptions.
title: US says it has targeted Iranian military facilities after responding to attacks on navy ships

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump's rejection of Iran 's ceasefire response as obstructing diplomacy, emphasizing "blistering" posts and "rages" while highlighting Netanyahu's uranium demands, whereas right-leaning outlets portray it as firm leadership via "fiery" rebukes and "lambasts," spotlighting Iran's "playing games" with nuclear transfers and reparations.
Pivotal divides emerge on Iran's maximalist terms and Israeli hawks, de-emphasized oppositely.
Center-Leaning sticks to neutral quotes like "totally unacceptable" without tone.
All agree on the response via mediators and Trump's vague dismissal, underscoring divides between hawkish resolve and diplomatic caution.
summary: President Trump officially rejected Iran’s latest diplomatic response on Sunday, May 10, 2026, characterized the counter-proposal as a continuation of "games" by the Iranian leadership.

In a statement posted to Truth Social, the President dismissed the formal reply from Tehran as "totally unacceptable," asserting that the Iranian government has spent decades using delay tactics and would "be laughing no longer" at the United States.

The Iranian counter-offer, delivered via Pakistani mediators, sought to pivot the negotiations toward a comprehensive end to the war on all regional fronts—including the conflict between Israel and Hezbollah—while demanding the immediate lifting of U.S. economic sanctions.

The U.S. proposal reportedly included ending the conflict, reopening the Strait of Hormuz and scaling back Iran’s nuclear program, while Trump accused Tehran of “playing games” with Washington for decades.

Iran has continued restricting traffic through the Strait of Hormuz as the U.S. military says it has blockaded Iranian ports and struck Iranian oil tankers, with Tehran warning of retaliation and pledging to protect its uranium facilities.
title: Trump calls Iran response to US proposal to end war 'totally unacceptable'

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame this tragedy through environmental factors, using language like "extreme heat" and "soared" to depict a "horror" linked to climate conditions.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage emphasizes the "Texas border," explicitly labeling the six victims as "migrants" to highlight an immigration crisis and "tragedies" involving smuggling.
While the left uses "upper-90s" temperatures to explain the deaths, the right employs terms like "multiple bodies" to underscore border insecurity.
Center-Leaning reports de-emphasize these narratives, prioritizing "confirmed" details like "mile marker 13.
All sides agree the discovery occurred in a Laredo railyard, reflecting broader ideological divides.
summary: Six people were found dead inside a Union Pacific cargo train at a railyard in Laredo on Sunday near 12100 Jim Young Way and Mile Marker 13.
Temperatures in Laredo hit 97 degrees on Sunday, with conditions inside train cars likely exceeding 100 degrees in the sweltering heat.

Laredo Police Department officers responded around 3 p.m. after a Union Pacific employee reported a "trailer box car with the discovery of multiple casualties in the car." The company spokesperson said they are "saddened by this incident."
The Webb County medical examiner will conduct autopsies to determine the cause and manner of death while the department continues its investigation; officials have not yet released victim identities.
Port Laredo accounted for 62% of Texas land port trade in 2024, valued at nearly $340 billion according to the Texas Comptroller, underscoring the city's critical role in regional commerce.
title: 6 people found dead inside a boxcar in Texas, officials say

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Trump's "rejects" of Iran 's "ceasefire" response as fueling market "jumps" and prolonged conflict volatility, emphasizing his agency in escalation with terms like "dryly rejecting," while right-leaning coverage portrays it as decisive dismissal of an "unacceptable" offer, highlighting stalled "nuclear talks" and Netanyahu's "not over" stance to underscore U.S. resolve and oil surge leverage.
Pivotal divides emerge on U.S. ammunition depletion and nuclear framing, de-emphasized by left perspectives.
Oil's 4% rise to $104 Brent/$98 WTI is touted as disruptive fallout on left, strategic gain on right.
Center coverage offers precise bilateral details without partisan tilt.
Amid shared oil surge and Hormuz concerns, divides reflect diplomacy-versus-strength debates on Iran.
summary: On Monday, oil prices surged more than 4% after President Donald Trump rejected Iran's peace proposal on Sunday, calling it "TOTALLY UNACCEPTABLE!" in a social media post. The rejection prolongs closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran's proposal submitted Sunday demanded sanctions relief, an end to the U.S. blockade, and sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz. The conflict, which began February 28, 2026, has effectively shut the waterway, disrupting one-fifth of global oil shipments.

Brent crude jumped 4.2% to $105.58 per barrel Monday morning, while Asian markets opened mixed. Senior market analyst Priyanka Sachdeva noted the market "continues to trade like a geopolitical headline machine" reacting to warnings from Washington and Tehran.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu warned on CBS News' "60 Minutes" Sunday that the war is "not over," citing the need to dismantle Iran's nuclear capabilities. The U.S. sea blockade of Iranian ports keeps the Strait effectively shut.

President Trump arrives in Beijing on Wednesday to discuss Iran with Chinese leader Xi Jinping. Analysts hope Beijing can leverage its influence to push Tehran toward a ceasefire and help reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
title: Oil Prices Jump After Donald Trump Dismisses Iran Proposal to End War

bias: Left-Leaning coverage highlights 'zero' US aid and 'tax dollars', framing Netanyahu's stance as timely fiscal relief while emphasizing Gulf state ties, whereas right-leaning outlets stress 'must become independent' with urgent, patriotic tones portraying self-reliance as a bold imperative.

Left employs dramatic phrases like 'It’s Time' for sensational appeal, right evokes sovereignty strength; pivotal divides emerge on de-emphasized Iran misjudgments, which right and center underscore versus left's focus on aid weaning.
The $3.8 billion annual aid is left's taxpayer burden but right's path to autonomy.
Center sticks to neutral 'draw down to zero' phrasing.
All agree on decade-long reduction to reset ties, reflecting ideological rifts over entanglement versus nationalism.
summary: On Sunday, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu told CBS News' '60 Minutes' he plans to phase out U.S. military financial support over the next decade, stating 'I want to draw down to zero the American financial support' currently worth $3.8 billion annually.

Congressional opposition to Israeli military aid has intensified, with Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez vowing last month to oppose all funding, as public sentiment shifted dramatically following the Gaza war outbreak in October 2023.

Israel currently receives $3.8 billion annually under a $38 billion U.S. commitment through 2028, now facing heightened political scrutiny; a March Pew survey found 60% of U.S. adults hold an unfavorable view of Israel, up nearly 20 points in four years.

Netanyahu attributed declining American support to what he claimed foreign countries have 'basically manipulated' on social media, stating this 'correlates almost 100%' with reduced backing, though he opposes censorship. He said President Donald Trump 's reaction was notably surprised.
title: Netanyahu wants Israel "to draw down to zero the American financial support"

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the hantavirus outbreak with dramatic terms like "hantavirus-stricken" and "hit," emphasizing chaotic evacuations of 17 Americans and latent threats despite no symptoms in the one positive case, evoking urgency and victimhood.
Right-Leaning coverage counters by stressing "deadly disease" and prior fatalities up to nine cases, portraying it as severe peril contained via biocontainment units.
Center-Leaning reports remain measured with "mildly positive," focusing clinical details without hype.
Pivotal divides emerge on outbreak scale and U.S. preparedness, de-emphasized by left.
All agree on repatriation to Omaha and monitoring, underscoring divides in risk perception versus resilience.
summary: On Sunday, the United States Department of Health and Human Services confirmed 17 Americans are being airlifted home from the MV Hondius cruise ship following a hantavirus outbreak near the Canary Islands.
The Andes strain of the virus can be fatal in up to 50% of cases, according to the World Health Organization , and has caused three deaths and sickened eight people with six confirmed cases.

Passengers are being transported to the ASPR Regional Emerging Special Pathogen Treatment Center at the University of Nebraska Medical Center in Omaha, Nebraska, with two suspected cases traveling in biocontainment units to prevent exposure.
Spain, France, Australia, Canada, the Netherlands, Turkey, the UK, and Ireland are also evacuating their nationals from the ship, which remains anchored near Tenerife in the Canary Islands.
Health authorities maintain the risk of the virus spreading is low, and WHO Director Maria Van Kerkhove stated, "We haven't been notified of any changes" to the strain's sequence.
title: One US citizen tests mildly positive for hantavirus, another has mild symptoms

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Germany's Tomahawk pursuit as "urgently trying" amid "worsening relations" and "dispute" with Trump, emphasizing desperation and overpaying to overcome political friction, while right-leaning coverage portrays it as a proactive "revives effort" after "cancellation" to address a defense "gap," highlighting strategic strengthening.
Left employs skeptical, alarmist tones critiquing alliances; right uses affirmative language evoking resolve.
Pivotal divides emerge on Trump-tied tensions and European deterrence shortfalls, de-emphasized by the right.
Center coverage neutrally "advances deals" without partisan edge.
All agree on reviving the July 2024 bid and Pistorius's Washington trip, underscoring divides over transatlantic trust versus hawkish priorities.
summary: The Financial Times reported Sunday that Germany is reviving its bid to purchase Tomahawk cruise missiles and mobile Typhon ground launchers from the United States, seeking Trump administration approval.

Depleted by the Iran war, U.S. military Tomahawk stockpiles have prompted Berlin to seek systems that strike targets hundreds of kilometers deep into enemy territory, a capability currently missing from Europe 's arsenal.
To revive Berlin's proposal, Minister of Defence Boris Pistorius plans to visit Washington, though the trip hinges on securing a meeting with U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.

A potential $5.5 billion order for the German-Norwegian submarine program is under discussion as German Vice Chancellor Lars Klingbeil visited Canada, where he met Prime Minister Mark Carney on Saturday to discuss deeper cooperation.

Bundestag President Julia Klöckner urged lawmakers to abandon expensive parliamentary expansions, such as the Luisenblock Ost project, stating, "when budgets are tight, we must not push ahead with expensive parliamentary expansions that we do not strictly need.
title: Germany revives effort to buy US Tomahawks, FT reports

summary: On Tuesday, a 37-year-old visitor from Seattle, Washington, allegedly threw a large rock at Lani, an endangered Hawaiian monk seal, while swimming off the West Maui coast.
Witness Kaylee Schnitzer reported the man threw a rock 'the size of a coconut' directly at the seal's head, dismissing her concerns by claiming he was wealthy enough to ignore potential fines.

Hawaii's Department of Land and Natural Resources confirmed on Wednesday that its Division of Conservation and Resources Enforcement detained the man shortly after the incident, referring the case to federal investigators.
Maui Mayor Richard Bissen condemned the act, vowing to ensure the suspect is 'prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law,' while the man declined to make a statement to authorities.
With only an estimated 1,400 to 1,600 Hawaiian monk seals remaining, the species is protected under the Endangered Species Act, which makes harassing the animals a federal crime .
title: Man throws rock at endangered monk seal in Maui

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Trump's announcement as a straightforward release of Polish and Moldovan citizens from detention, using neutral phrasing like "announces release," while right-leaning coverage portrays him as a decisive broker, emphasizing terms like "brokers," "helped secure," and dramatic "captivity" to highlight diplomatic triumphs.

Right employs triumphant tones with "delivers for our Allies," contrasting left's de-emphasis on details like special envoy John Coale, thanks to Lukashenko, and months of negotiations—pivotal lines triggering right's exaltation versus left's restraint.
Both tout the five prisoners' freedom, though right celebrates it as a win; center attributes via "Trump says" for balance.
Agreement on the core release underscores ideological rifts over Trump's foreign policy prowess.
summary: On Sunday, President Donald Trump announced that three Polish nationals and two Moldovan prisoners were released from Belarusian and Russian detention following a negotiated deal.

Special Presidential Envoy John Coale led American negotiations to finalize the deal, which Trump said began last September when Polish President Karol Nawrocki requested assistance securing Andrzej Poczobut's freedom.
Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk confirmed Poczobut's release on April 28 as part of a prisoner swap, marking the conclusion of months of diplomatic negotiations between Warsaw and Minsk.

Trump thanked Belarusian President Aleksandr Lukashenko for his "cooperation and friendship" while crediting Coale for "pushing hard" to finalize the deal, as the United States reportedly began removing sanctions against Belarus.

The five freed individuals are undergoing debriefing and medical evaluations before reuniting with families in Poland and Moldova, following a recurring pattern of diplomatic exchanges involving Belarus.
title: Trump says Polish, Moldovan prisoners released from Belarusian, Russian detention

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame BBC shelving of a Gaza documentary as "censorship" and "silencing," portraying filmmakers' BAFTA win and defiant "calls out" as righteous resistance to suppression.
Right-Leaning coverage emphasizes winners "lashing out" with "furious remarks" in a "tense speech" over an "impartiality row," dramatizing emotional confrontation against the broadcaster.
Pivotal divides arise in left amplification of explicit bias accusations and daring the BBC to cut their speech, de-emphasized by the right's focus on outrage.
Both agree on the award, speech criticism, and impartiality-driven drop, underscoring ideological rifts over media neutrality and Palestinian narratives.
summary: On Sunday, filmmakers behind Gaza: Doctors Under Attack won a BAFTA TV Award and used their acceptance speech to publicly rebuke the BBC for previously shelving the documentary.

The BBC originally commissioned the documentary but dropped it in June 2025, citing impartiality concerns over journalist Ramita Navai's radio interview and social media activity, per then-executive Deborah Turness.

During the ceremony, Navai claimed the film reveals 'Israel has killed over 47,000 children and women in Gaza,' while executive producer Ben de Pear challenged the BBC: 'Given you dropped our film, will you drop us from the BAFTA screening?'

Following the BBC's rejection, Channel 4 broadcast the documentary, with head of news Louisa Compton calling it a 'meticulously reported and important film' exemplifying the network's commitment to brave journalism.
The documentary investigates alleged attacks on medical facilities in Gaza, with Navai stating the film shows 'Israel has been killing the very people trying to keep healthcare system alive.
title: Gaza documentary dropped by the BBC wins BAFTA

bias: Right-Leaning outlets frame the event as a "shocker" where a "biological male" "dominates" or "steamrolls" competition, using phrases like "Save Girls Sports " to emphasize "fury.
Conversely, left-leaning narratives prioritize "trans inclusion," portraying the athlete’s three-event victory as "equal athletic participation.
This divide centers on whether policies uphold "inclusion" or "undermine Title IX.
While the right highlights the three wins as proof of "physical superiority," center-leaning coverage de-emphasizes national political connections, neutrally noting the performance "ignited" debate.
Both sides agree on the athlete’s three wins, reflecting a broader clash between sex-based protections and gender-identity rights.
summary: On Saturday, the California Family Council protested outside Yorba Linda High School ahead of the CIF Southern Section Division 3 Track and Field Preliminaries, where Senior AB Hernandez of Jurupa Valley High School won the long jump, high jump, and triple jump events.
Critics argue CIF bylaws allowing participation based on gender identity disadvantage female competitors and conflict with federal Title IX protections for women in athletics.

The U.S. Justice Department sued California for alleged Title IX violations, while Republican gubernatorial candidates Steve Hilton and Riverside County Sheriff Chad Bianco pledged to repeal Assembly Bill 1266.

Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom's office characterized the attacks as politically motivated, stating he rejects efforts to "weaponize this debate" while emphasizing fairness and respect for all students.

Gabrielle Antolovich, board president and CEO at the Billy DeFrank LGBTQ+ Community Center, argues criticism of transgender athletes is often misplaced; Hernandez previously told CBS News she is "just a normal kid going to school, playing sports .
title: Protesters challenge trans sports policy in California
NIST Reports New Big G Value After Decade-Long Torsion-Balance Study

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the $15 million lawsuit as a defense of personal rights against corporate exploitation, using language like "disrespectful and insensitive" to highlight Samsung’s alleged overreach.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets emphasize the "15 million dollars" figure to suggest celebrity excess, while using terms like "appropriated" and "sales hit" to focus on property violations.
A key dividing line is Samsung’s conduct; the left highlights ignored requests, while the right describes their reaction as "abominably" handled.
Center-Leaning reports differ by detailing copyright ownership.
Both sides agree on the unauthorized image use, reflecting broader tensions between individual autonomy and corporate power.
summary: On Friday, singer-songwriter Dua Lipa sued Samsung in the Central District of California, alleging the electronics manufacturer used her likeness on television packaging without permission or payment.

Samsung allegedly began placing the photograph—taken backstage at the 2024 Austin City Limits Festival—on TV boxes last year as part of a large-scale retail marketing campaign without the artist's approval.

Seeking at least $15 million in damages, Lipa asserts copyright infringement, trademark violations, and false endorsement under the Lanham Act, claiming Samsung created a false impression of partnership.
After Lipa discovered the unauthorized use, she demanded the company cease distribution, but the suit claims Samsung responded in a "dismissive and callous" manner and continued the campaign.

The filing emphasizes that Lipa maintains a "premium brand" and is "highly selective" with endorsements, while Twitter posts suggest some consumers purchased TVs based on the unauthorized apparent association.
title: Dua Lipa Sues Samsung for $15 Million Alleging Unauthorised Use of Her Image on TV Packaging

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the legacy as a "forceful advocate" for civil rights, emphasizing minority protection.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets highlight his history as a "hidden child," portraying his survival as providing "unique gravitas" and a "towering" stature.
While center-leaning reports note his "transformative" leadership as a "Jewish pope," both they and the left de-emphasize his personal Holocaust history.
All perspectives highlight his death at 86, though the left presents his three-decade tenure as a virtue of advocacy while the right frames it as a biographical detail.
These narratives converge on his influence regarding Holocaust memory and the struggle against antisemitism .
summary: On Sunday, Abraham 'Abe' Foxman, a towering figure who led the Anti-Defamation League for nearly three decades, died at age 86, the ADL confirmed.

Born to Polish Jewish parents in present-day Belarus in 1940, Foxman survived the Holocaust after his parents entrusted him to a Catholic nanny who baptized him, eventually immigrating to the United States in 1950.
Spending his entire 50-year career with the group, Foxman served as national director from 1987 to 2015, counseling presidents and diplomats while building a formidable research arm into extremists.

Israeli President Isaac Herzog praised Foxman as a 'passionate Zionist, a humanist, and an outspoken, wise friend,' while Jonathan Greenblatt, ADL's current director, said he was 'an iconic Jewish leader' who transformed the organization.

Foxman will be buried on Tuesday in New York, having remained active since retiring and criticizing the Democratic Party's shift on Israel while asserting, 'If Israel ceases to be an open democracy, I won't be able to support it.
title: Abe Foxman, Holocaust Survivor Who Led Adl for Nearly Three Decades, Dies at 86

bias: Across left and right coverage, the rare-earths agreement’s continuity is anchored to a “US official says” frame, but the extension outlook splits the narratives: center emphasizes that an extension will be announced “at the appropriate time,” while right stresses “it wasn’t clear” whether any extension will happen, pairing that uncertainty with a “Trump-Xi meeting” linkage tied to trade-tension and rare-earths status.
Left-Leaning items de-emphasize geopolitical timing cues and instead keep the focus on the US-spokesperson confirmation and “still in effect” continuity language.
Despite these tonal divergences—reassurance versus ambiguity—both sides converge on the shared baseline: the deal had not yet expired at reporting time.
summary: A rare earths agreement between the United States and China remains in effect, according to a senior US official.
US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping are scheduled to meet in Beijing on May 14 and 15 for high-level talks.
The upcoming summit will address issues including the US-Israeli war on Iran, trade tensions, and Taiwan.
The leaders last met in October in South Korea and agreed to pause a damaging trade war involving tariffs and threats to rare earth supplies.
title: Rare earths deal between US and China is still in effect, US official says

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the recovery within large-scale military exercises, emphasizing the human cost of deployments while de-emphasizing off-duty details.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage highlights "war games" as necessary, using emotive language like "tragic" and "lifeless" to describe the "accident.
A pivotal dividing line is the rescue attempt; right-leaning reports detail a soldier jumping in to save another, framing it as heroism.
Center-Leaning reports focus on the "recreational hike" context.
While all sides agree on the 27-year-old’s identity, the right highlights the search for a "2nd soldier" to emphasize persistence, reflecting broader ideological divides over military presence.
summary: Military recovery teams found the remains of 1st Kendrick Lamont Key Jr. in the Atlantic Ocean on Saturday, May 9. Search efforts continue for the second American soldier who disappeared near Morocco's coast last weekend.
The soldiers disappeared near the Cap Draa Training Area on the night of May 2 while off duty. Troops were hiking near oceanside cliffs when one fell, prompting a failed rescue attempt by the second.

More than 600 personnel from the United States, Morocco, and other partners have joined the search-and-rescue operation. Teams have covered 12,000 square kilometers of sea and littoral zone, currently adding around 3,000 square kilometers daily.

A U.S. military element remains in Morocco after the African Lion 26 exercise ended Friday to "provide command and control and continue search and rescue operations," according to the Southern European Task Force.

Key served as an Air Defense Artillery officer assigned to Charlie Battery, 5th Battalion, 4th Air Defense Artillery Regiment. He earned his commission in 2024 following graduation from Officer Candidate School.
title: Remains of US soldier who went missing during military exercises in Morocco have been recovered

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the talks as neutral "economic and trade consultations" led by China's vice premier, de-emphasizing US itineraries, while right-leaning ones portray US Treasury moves to "boost" alliances "amid rising tensions" ahead of a Trump-Xi summit, highlighting "President Trump" diplomacy and "U.S. Economy" strength with urgent "NEW:" alerts.
Right employs threat-infused, action-oriented tones contrasting left's procedural restraint.
Pivotal divides: de-emphasized Treasury Japan/Korea visits and economic boasts.
Center adds balanced prior meeting timelines.
All converge on May 12-13 dates and mutual trade focus, exposing rifts in US-centric leverage versus cooperative portrayals.
summary: Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng will lead a delegation to South Korea for economic and trade talks with the United States from May 12 to 13, as announced by the Ministry of Commerce.
The talks will follow the consensus reached by Chinese President Xi Jinping and US President Donald Trump during their previous meetings and focus on mutual economic and trade issues.
US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent confirmed he will meet Vice Premier He Lifeng in Seoul before attending the leaders' summit in Beijing between Presidents Xi and Trump.
The upcoming summit aims to address trade tensions, Taiwan issues, and broader global concerns.
title: China's He to hold trade talks with US delegation in South Korea

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the tragedy as a "mass shooting" killing eight children, emphasizing victim funerals and injured mothers viewing bodies to evoke communal grief and subtle calls for reform, while right-leaning ones highlight "monster dad" "murdering in cold blood," spotlighting familial betrayal and perpetrator depravity for outrage over moral failure.
Pivotal divides emerge in the father's role—demonized intensely on the right, de-emphasized on the left—and predawn horror, amplifying right-wing condemnation.
Center coverage neutrally stresses community honors like half-staff flags.
All agree on the Shreveport funeral for seven siblings and a cousin slain April 19, underscoring divides between systemic gun critiques and individual accountability.
summary: On Saturday, mourners gathered at Summer Grove Baptist Church in Shreveport for funeral services honoring eight children killed in an April 19 mass shooting.
Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry ordered U.S. and state flags flown at half-staff through next week across the State Capitol and government buildings to honor the victims.

Among those in attendance was former U.S. Rep. Gabby Giffords, whose political career was cut short by a 2011 assassination attempt. Speakers acknowledged that "there are no words sufficient to ease this pain."
Mourners filed past eight white caskets while gospel singer Kim Burrell and Bishop Bernard Kimble led prayers for healing and strength. Attendees wore pink, purple, and blue in honor of the children.
The investigation into the April 19 attack remains ongoing, as the event stands as the deadliest mass shooting in the United States in more than two years.
title: 8 children killed in Louisiana mass shooting remembered at funeral

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the TikToker's crowdfunding as an optimistic grassroots revolt with phrases like "thousands support" and "pledged to save," playfully evoking "get in loser" to romanticize public revival against corporate woes, while right-leaning ones portray it as entrepreneurial triumph via "viral TikToker rallies" and "racks up" millions, hailing it a "genius idea" for bypassing failed institutions.

Pivotal divides emerge on government bailout denial, spotlighted rightward as failure, versus left emphasis on Spirit's deceptive past; pledges from $214 million to $337 million are touted left as heroic scale, right as explosive momentum.
Center coverage neutrally notes figures like $300M, de-emphasizing hype.
Both sides agree on viral pledges topping hundreds of millions, underscoring populist versus capitalist divides in economic agency.
summary: Voice actor Hunter Peterson launched a viral TikTok campaign seeking to crowd-fund the revival of Spirit Airlines , with his website letsbuyspirit.com attracting over 156,000 non-binding pledges toward a $1.75 billion target.
Spirit Airlines ceased operations on Saturday after 34 years due to financial losses and rising fuel costs. The carrier is being liquidated in bankruptcy court while lenders repossess its aircraft.

Industry analysts warn that Spirit needs at least $1 billion to settle debts and resume operations, yet the $132 million in non-binding pledges represents only a fraction of capital required. The average pledge stands at $772.

Any revival faces massive Department of Transportation regulatory hurdles, including a rigorous "fitness" test and a requirement that at least 75 percent of voting interest be held by Americans. A crowdfunded airline model has no historical precedent.

Economists note the "Spirit Effect"—where ultra-low-cost carriers lower fares across the board—is at risk without the airline. Peterson described the viral campaign as "a joke that is rapidly going out of control," yet it underscores consumer frustration over rising travel costs.
title: Viral Campaign to Revive Spirit Airlines Draws $337M in Pledges and Union Backing

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the project through "protesting" residents "livid" and "furious" over its "massive" scale—"twice Manhattan's size"—and ecological threats like soaring energy prices and water scarcity, portraying O'Leary as "defending" jobs.

Right-Leaning outlets counter by emphasizing the data center's role to "rival China's tech dominance" and "generate" off-grid natural gas power as a national security triumph, depicting "enraged" locals chanting "Shame!" as overreactions.
Center coverage neutrally balances backlash with investment potential.
Pivotal divides emerge on U.S.-China rivalry, de-emphasized leftward.
All agree on unanimous approval amid protests and massive energy-water strains, underscoring ecological versus economic-nationalist tensions.
summary: Shark Tank investor Kevin O'Leary is pushing back against public opposition to his Stratos data center project in Box Elder County, Utah, which received approval last month.

Military Installation Development Authority executive director Paul Morris confirmed the site will rely on natural gas, scaling to 9 gigawatts of power compared with Utah's 4 gigawatts of average annual consumption.
Citing threats to the shrinking Great Salt Lake, protesters gathered with signs reading "People before profits" to express concerns over local resource strain.
Dismissing opposition as manufactured, Leary claimed most protesters were outsiders and emphasized that "sustainability is at the heart of what we do" through improved technology .
The conflict illustrates rising friction between AI infrastructure expansion and community resistance, as similar projects in Alberta and Utah face growing scrutiny over environmental impacts.
title: Utah Residents Seek Referendum to Block $100B AI Data Center

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame "huge ice block" hazards and "undeterred" climbers as reckless elitism fueling "traffic jams," while right-leaning ones highlight resilient perseverance "despite" high fees and a "record 492 permits" as economic triumph, emphasizing expedition leaders' calls for "extreme caution" from fatal ice histories.
Left employs alarmist tones on overcrowding inequality; right uses triumphant language on human grit.
Pivotal divides include right-exclusive fatal precedents and left's de-emphasized revenue over $7 million, which center neutrally balances with mild "warming" alarms.
All agree on the late season opening and permit surge, underscoring sustainability versus achievement divides.
summary: On Tuesday, May 5, Pemba Tenduk Sherpa, a guide with Seven Summit Treks, and Nimish Kumar Singh, a client with Pioneer Adventure, were injured when a serac collapsed in the Khumbu Icefall.

The collapse occurred shortly before 6 a.m. during the busy early morning rush when climbers traditionally seek cooler, safer conditions; climate change has made the Khumbu Icefall's seracs and snow bridges increasingly unstable.
Nearby Sherpas rescued the two men from the debris, and a helicopter from Lukla airport arrived by 6:15 a.m., transporting both to HAMS hospital in Kathmandu for treatment.
Both climbers remain stable and are expected to recover, with sources at Seven Summit Treks expressing hope that Sherpa will be discharged from the hospital within 24 hours.
Separately, Lakpa Tendi Sherpa, a guide with Seven Summit Treks, died following a fatal fall at 5,200m above Gorak Shep on May 5, 2026; no details about how the fall occurred have been provided.
title: Two Injured in Mt. Everest Khumbu Icefall Serac Collapse

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Bahrain 's "rounds up dozens" with "alleged" IRGC links as aggressive overreach, emphasizing Syrian support while evoking authoritarian skepticism.

Right-Leaning coverage counters with urgent "NEW" "crackdown" on "ties to Iranian Military Influence," highlighting UAE and Egypt backing to portray hawkish triumphs against subversion in Shia communities.
Center cautiously notes "terrorist network" claims amid "41" arrests and rights groups' "crack down on dissent" warnings.
Pivotal divides emerge over UAE/Egypt solidarity versus dissent suppression.
All agree on "41" IRGC-linked arrests and Saudi support, underscoring human rights caution versus anti-Iran geopolitical solidarity.
summary: On Saturday, Bahraini authorities announced the arrest of 41 individuals alleged to be the "core" of a terrorist network linked to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps , with legal proceedings currently underway.

These arrests follow investigations into espionage and sympathy for Iranian attacks, which have repeatedly targeted Gulf States' civilian and energy infrastructure since the regional conflict began in late February.

Authorities previously detained four citizens on March 12 and five on March 15 for sharing sensitive information with the IRGC, including coordinates of vital locations captured using high-resolution photographic equipment.

The United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia publicly affirmed solidarity with Bahrain's security measures, reinforcing a coordinated Gulf response to alleged Iranian influence and intensifying regional pressure.

Bahrain revoked the nationality of 69 people accused of "colluding with foreign entities," while the London-based Bahrain Institute for Rights and Democracy termed the measure "dangerous" amid ongoing security crackdowns.
title: Bahrain Arrests 41 Over Alleged Links to Iran's IRGC

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the visit through speculative alarmism, using phrases like " Iran war " to suggest diplomatic regression.
They emphasize the trip’s short three-day duration as evidence of a "chillier" relationship.
Conversely, right-leaning perspectives portray this cooling as necessary "toughness," focusing on a mission to "secure deals" despite China’s "economic ties" to Iran.
Center-Leaning coverage highlights the disconnect between Trump’s "big, fat hug" rhetoric and the somber diplomatic reality.
While all sides agree the 2017 "fanfare" has vanished, they diverge on whether this shift signals leadership failure or a pragmatic pivot in geopolitical strategy.
summary: President Donald Trump is preparing for a visit to China to meet Xi Jinping , but the diplomatic atmosphere is expected to be more tense than during Trump’s 2017 trip because of ongoing trade disputes and the broader geopolitical fallout from the Iran conflict.

China’s relationship with Iran has become a key issue ahead of the meeting. Washington wants Beijing to use its influence with Tehran — especially regarding oil trade and stability in the Strait of Hormuz — while China is balancing its economic ties with Iran against its broader relationship with the U.S.

Despite tensions, both governments appear interested in keeping high-level dialogue open. Analysts cited in the article suggest each side sees strategic value in continued engagement, though expectations for major breakthroughs on trade, security, or regional disputes remain limited.
title: Iran war could make Trump’s trip to China a bit chillier than his first-term visit

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes swift ten-minute containment and staff safety with reassuring neutrality, de-emphasizing disruptions, while right-leaning outlets amplify "massive" and "powerful" explosions "shaking" homes via urgent tones around "emergency crews" and sheriff probes.
Center perspectives highlight community impacts like school lockdowns and road closures, bridging scales without partisan flair.
Pivotal divides emerge on response efficiency versus investigative scrutiny, framing the same no-injuries event—one as contained win, the other as looming crisis.
All align on the explosion, fire, and smoke plume, underscoring ideological splits over energy sector resilience versus vulnerability.
summary: On Friday afternoon, a powerful explosion and fire occurred at the Chalmette Refinery in St. Bernard Parish, with officials confirming no injuries were reported and all employees accounted for.

Located roughly five miles east of downtown New Orleans along the Mississippi River, the facility processes approximately 189,000 barrels of crude oil per day and triggered a large response from the Bernard Fire Department.
The blast, which occurred just before 1 p.m., forced the brief closure of eastbound lanes on West St. Bernard Highway from Jackson Boulevard to Palmisano Boulevard; nearby schools remain on lockdown.

Landry posted on social media that he is monitoring developments and working closely with parish leaders to ensure public safety, while the St. Bernard Parish Sheriff urged motorists to avoid the area.

While road closures have since reopened, Chalmette Refining stated that appropriate government agencies were notified and the cause of the incident remains under investigation as emergency crews continue containment work.
title: Refinery Blast Near New Orleans Deepens US Fuel Crisis

summary: The White House called Mark Hamill "one sick individual" after he posted an AI-generated image of President Donald Trump in a shallow grave on social media.
The image showed Trump lying with eyes closed next to a gravestone inscribed "Donald J. Trump 1946-2024" with the words "If Only" above or written on it.
Hamill stated he wished Trump would live long enough to be held accountable for alleged crimes and political defeat.
Hamill later deleted the post and apologized, saying he actually wished Trump the opposite of death but apologized if the image was inappropriate.
title: Mark Hamill Clarifies Trump Grave Image After White House Rebuke
IDF Reservist Killed in Hezbollah Drone Attack Near Lebanon Border

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the bill as an aggressive "push to scrap entirely" the Oslo Accords, portraying them as a "cornerstone" of peace while labeling the sponsor "far-right" pejoratively to evoke obstructionism.
Right-Leaning coverage, conversely, emphasizes resolute "cancel" actions, highlighting Oslo's role in "decades of terrorism " and security erosion for a corrective tone.
Pivotal divides emerge over Oslo's legacy—peace milestone versus terror enabler—and settlement expansion, de-emphasized by the left.
Center-Leaning reports echo "scrap" and "block" rhetoric but spotlight settlements.
All agree on the far-right sponsor's proposal to revoke Oslo and halt a Palestinian state, underscoring ideological rifts on sovereignty versus two-state reconciliation.
summary: On Sunday, an Israeli ministerial committee will review a far-right-sponsored bill to repeal the 1993 Oslo Accords, submitted by Knesset Deputy Speaker Limor Son Har-Melech.

The 1993 agreement serves as the cornerstone of the two-state solution, which sought to end Israel 's occupation and create an independent Palestinian state, with West Bank governance divided into Areas A, B and C.

Har-Melech, a member of National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir's Otzma Yehudit , called the bill "a first and necessary step" to encourage settlement in Areas A and B and repeal the "disastrous Oslo Accords."

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has long opposed the accords, boasting in 2001 about "putting an end" to them, and recently said he was "proud" of preventing the establishment of a Palestinian state.

If approved by the committee, the bill proceeds to the Knesset Praesidium for scrutiny, aligning with broader government efforts to undermine the accords through settlement expansion in the occupied West Bank.
title: Israeli Bill Seeks to Scrap Oslo Accords, Block Palestinian State

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize a "barrage of bombings" and "no letup" in aggression, humanizing the toll by highlighting a "12-year-old girl" to portray an "alleged ceasefire.

Conversely, right-leaning perspectives frame events as responses to Hezbollah, employing skeptical language like "claims" regarding casualties or contextualizing strikes within a broader "US-Israel-Iran" struggle.

While center-leaning coverage prioritizes attribution to the "health ministry" and notes the "escalation" before diplomatic talks, all sides acknowledge the strikes occurred despite a truce, reflecting deep-seated divides over military justification versus humanitarian cost within a volatile geopolitical landscape.
summary: An Israeli strike on the southern Lebanese town of Saksakiyeh killed at least seven people, including a child, and wounded 15 on Saturday, Lebanon's health ministry said.
Israel said it had struck Hezbollah militants in the town, accusing them of planning attacks against Israeli soldiers.
Lebanon and Israel have continued to exchange fire in southern Lebanon despite a U.S.-brokered ceasefire announced on April 16.
title: Lebanon says Israeli strike on south kills seven including child

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the Trump-Xi summit amid "war against Iran" and "dispute over Taiwan's sovereignty," emphasizing fragile tensions with terms like "fragile trade truce," while right-leaning coverage highlights Trump's resolve to "apply pressure" on Xi over China's Iran approach and oil sales, portraying assertive leadership.
Pivotal divides emerge on Iran—left as U.S. aggression, right as justified confrontation—and Taiwan, de-emphasized by right.
Center coverage neutrally queries tariff status, focusing facts over drama.
All agree on stabilizing trade-strained ties via Iran talks, underscoring ideological clashes on American strength versus global frictions.
summary: Trump and Xi are set to hold talks from May 13-15 in China, the first US presidential visit in nearly a decade, amid escalating trade tensions between the world's two largest economies.
The visit will mark a key test in the fragile trade truce between the US and China, with Trump having previously imposed tariffs on $250 billion worth of Chinese imports in 2018.

While Biden has kept up pressure on Beijing by maintaining Trump's tariffs and introducing restrictions on Chinese firms like Huawei and TikTok, the talks aim to address a range of issues including Iran, nuclear weapons, trade, and artificial intelligence.
title: Trump and Xi set for talks spanning Iran, nuclear, trade and AI
Report: FBI Expands Leak Investigation Linked to Gabbard Intercept

summary: Yellowstone County District Judge Thomas Pardy ruled against Rep. Amy Regier and the Montana Life Defense Fund, rejecting their challenge to the ballot language of Constitutional Initiative 128, the state's abortion protection amendment.
Challenging the ballot language, the fund argued the amendment was invalid because the full text was not printed on the ballot, a claim the Montana Supreme Court had previously dismissed.
The judge noted that the Montana Constitution does not require the full text on the ballot, reasoning that if the framers had intended such a requirement, they would have stated it.
Montanans approved the measure in November 2024 with 345,070 votes in favor and 252,300 opposed, a result Akilah Deernose, executive director of the ACLU of Montana, says this ruling helps uphold.

It remains unclear whether Regier and the fund will appeal Pardy's decision to the state's highest court, though this marks the third time courts have dismissed such challenges to the abortion protection law.
title: Montana Judge Rejects Challenge to Abortion Amendment Ballot Text
